metadata.title: Pick a Botspeak Prompt & Nail 'Specific Enough'
metadata.topic: INFO 7375 BOTSPEAK PROMPT ADAPTATION — E1/5 — DOMAIN ADAPTATION (21 pts)
metadata.playlist: The Botspeak Prompt Adaptation — INFO 7375 Computational Skepticism for AI
metadata.source: INFO 7375 (Computational Skepticism for AI) — Botspeak Prompt Adaptation (100 pts)

beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam, in for Bear. This series is five episodes on one assignment: the Botspeak Prompt Adaptation for INFO 7375. A hundred points — eighty on the rubric, twenty on your relative quartile against the class. The rubric has six deliverables and you earn them in order. The biggest single component is twenty-one points: the domain-adapted prompt. Here is what the assignment is actually testing: how precisely you name your role. 'Healthcare professional' describes a thousand jobs. 'Clinical pharmacist on a night shift reviewing discharge medications' describes one. The whole grade turns on which one you write. Episode one has one job: pick a Botspeak exercise prompt and write a role statement tight enough to pass. Let's go.

props.segment: Pick a Botspeak Prompt & Nail 'Specific Enough'
props.command: Pick a Botspeak LLM Exercise (Ch 0–13). Write a bounded role statement that passes the adjacent-profession test.
props.runningText: the whole grade turns on how precisely you name your role…
output: Chosen Botspeak exercise prompt — verbatim, chapter noted
Bounded role statement — one sentence, adjacent-profession test passed

beats.act: what is a Botspeak exercise prompt
beats.narration_text: Botspeak's chapters zero through thirteen each end with LLM exercises — prompts designed for a generic professional role, with bracketed fields where your specific content goes. Fields like: the role, the task, the context, the output format. Your assignment is to take one of those exercises and make it yours — replace every bracket with content specific to your actual professional domain. First move: pick the right exercise. The right one is a prompt whose underlying task — the kind of work it asks the model to help with — actually occurs in your professional life. If you are a nurse, pick the exercise about clinical documentation review, not the one about financial modeling. The exercise is raw material. You are going to replace the brackets with your actual job.

props.artifactTitle: What Is a Botspeak Exercise Prompt?
props.artifactHeading: A generic template — yours to make specific
artifactLines: Botspeak Ch 0–13: each chapter ends with LLM Exercise prompts.
Structure: bracketed fields — [role], [task], [context], [output format].
Your job: replace every bracket with role-specific content.
Pick the exercise whose underlying task occurs in your actual professional life.
The exercise is raw material — the brackets are the work.

beats.narration_text: The 'specific enough' test has one question: if you handed this role statement to someone at your workplace, would they immediately recognize the exact job — or could it describe five adjacent roles? 'Healthcare professional' fails. It could be a physician, a nurse, a pharmacist, a billing coordinator. 'Clinical pharmacist on a night shift reviewing discharge medications before a patient leaves the unit' passes. Three things make a role statement specific enough. The role is bounded — one job, not a category. The task context is named — not just what you do but when and under what pressure. And the failure mode is visible — a specific enough role tells you what going wrong looks like in this exact situation. Tighten until the description uniquely fits you.

shot.description: Split-column comparison. Left: 'TOO GENERIC' — 'Healthcare professional' in muted ink, with a list of five adjacent roles it could describe (physician, nurse, pharmacist, billing coordinator, care coordinator). Each labeled with a soft gray tag. Right: 'SPECIFIC ENOUGH' — 'Clinical pharmacist, night shift, reviewing discharge medications before patient leaves unit' in terracotta ink. Three checkmarks: Bounded role / Named context / Visible failure mode. A center divider line. Claude palette: cream background #FAF9F5, ink #3D3929, terracotta accent #D97757.

beats.narration_text: The adjacent profession test is binary: take your role statement and swap it into a neighbor's job. If it still fits, it is not specific enough. A clinical pharmacist's neighbor is the clinical nurse. Can a night-shift nurse claim your role statement? If yes, tighten. Here is the procedure: write your role statement. List two adjacent professions — people who do similar but distinct work. Try to give your role statement to each of them. If either can claim it, identify the phrase doing insufficient work and replace it with something only you would write. Do this once before you submit anything. One pass of this test is worth ten minutes of re-reading the statement yourself.

shot.description: Three-node diagram. Center: a card with 'Your Role Statement' and a placeholder role statement in italic serif. Left: 'Neighbor 1' node connected by a dashed arrow labeled 'Does this fit them?'. Right: 'Neighbor 2' node connected by a dashed arrow labeled 'Does this fit them?'. Below: a verdict row — if either arrow says YES → 'Tighten: find the generic phrase, replace it'; if both say NO → 'Pass: statement is specific enough.' Terracotta accent on the YES/tighten path. Claude palette.

beats.narration_text: Once you have your role statement, go through the exercise prompt bracket by bracket. List every field: role, context, task, constraints, output format, stakeholders — whatever the exercise uses. Next to each field, write what goes in it from your actual work. Not 'healthcare-related tasks.' Not 'relevant stakeholders.' Named precisely: 'a pharmacy technician who will fill the prescription in the next fifteen minutes,' 'a discharge order that includes a high-alert medication,' 'a Beers Criteria flag the attending may have missed.' If you can write generic content into a field, the field is not done. Every bracket you fill generically is a point you leave on the table in the Domain Adaptation component.

props.artifactHeading: List every field — fill each with precision
artifactLines: [role] → one specific job title + situational context
[task] → the exact decision or document you produce
[context] → the pressure, timeline, or constraint it occurs under
[stakeholders] → named precisely — who reads this output and acts on it
[output format] → the exact artifact: a memo, a flag, a five-bullet checklist
Rule: if a neighbor could claim your fill, it is not specific enough yet.

beats.narration_text: Two traps kill this deliverable. The first: the role is too big. 'Software engineer' is a whole profession. 'Backend engineer on the payments team, reviewing a pull request that touches the fraud-detection service' is a job. The scope of the role sets the scope of everything downstream — your constraints, your exclusions, your failure modes. A big role produces generic everything that follows. The second trap: picking a prompt that does not fit your domain. If you pick a Botspeak exercise about market research and your work is in clinical nursing, the mismatch compounds at every stage. Pick the exercise that most naturally fits the actual decisions you make. If nothing fits cleanly, pick the closest one and explain the translation in your justification — that transparency is its own evidence of rigor.

artifactLines: TRAP 1 — Role too big: 'Software engineer' = a profession, not a job.
Fix: scope to one task in one situational context under one kind of pressure.
TRAP 2 — Wrong exercise: a market-research prompt for a clinical nurse compounds at every stage.
Fix: pick the exercise whose underlying task actually occurs in your work.
If nothing fits cleanly: pick closest, explain the translation in your justification.
That transparency is evidence of rigor — not a deduction.

beats.narration_text: The deliverable for this episode is two things. First: the chosen Botspeak exercise prompt, verbatim, with its source chapter noted. Second: a bounded role statement — one or two sentences — that passes the adjacent-profession test. That is it. No adapted prompt yet — that is episode three. No five-component spec yet — that is episode two. This is the raw material that every downstream deliverable is built from. If the role statement is vague, the adaptation is vague, the justification is vague, the exclusions are vague. Get this tight now. Everything that follows is a function of what you write here.

props.artifactHeading: Two things — no more, no less
artifactLines: 1. The chosen Botspeak exercise prompt — verbatim, source chapter noted.
2. A bounded role statement — 1–2 sentences, adjacent-profession test passed.
Not the adapted prompt yet (E3). Not the spec yet (E2).
This is the foundation. Every downstream component is a function of this.
Vague here → vague everywhere downstream.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: 'I am writing my role statement for the Botspeak Prompt Adaptation. My professional domain is [your domain]. Here is my role statement draft: [your draft]. Run the adjacent-profession test. First, name three adjacent professions this statement could describe. Then tell me which part of the statement is doing the most work to distinguish me — and which part is still generic enough that a neighbor could claim it. Then suggest one specific addition that would make the statement fail the adjacent-profession test for all three neighbors.' Read what it surfaces. The phrase the model says a neighbor could claim — that is your revision target. Fix it before you move to episode two.

props.segment: Pressure-test your role statement before you build on it
props.command: Run the adjacent-profession test on my role statement draft. Name three adjacent professions it could describe. Identify what's still generic. Suggest one addition that makes it fail the test for all three neighbors.
props.runningText: paste this into Claude — fix the generic phrase before episode 2…
output: Three adjacent professions the draft could describe
The phrase doing insufficient distinguishing work
One specific addition that closes the gap

beats.narration_text: Pick a Botspeak Prompt and Nail Specific Enough. Liam, in for Bear.
